content:
37 min read
Just now

--

Most token-saving advice on the internet is folklore. This is what the data from 573 actual sessions says — including which famous tactics are wrong.
Abstract
For readers who want the results without the derivations.
This article measures token usage on Claude Code against 28,738 assistant requests drawn from 573 real sessions of one developer, recorded January to May 2026 on the model generation available at the time — Opus 4.6 and 4.7, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5, with every controlled experiment run on Opus 4.7 specifically — cross-checked with Anthropic’s official documentation. Read every number here as a snapshot of that generation, not of whatever model you are running today: the 15× Opus-to-Haiku cost ratio, the 2.1% / 3.3% / 4.4% tool-error rates, and the Opus-4.7 tokenizer note are all Opus 4.7 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5 measurements. Opus 4.8 and Sonnet 5 shipped after this measurement window closed and were never tested here; nothing below should be taken as describing how they behave. The single largest finding is that your bill is dominated by invisible architecture, not your prompts. A typical one-line prompt is roughly 0.5% of what’s already in context at session start, so prompt-engineering advice has small effect next to configuration-level changes. The biggest lever by far is model selection: Opus generated 88% of spend at near-equal call count to Haiku, an effective 15× per-call cost ratio. Default to Sonnet for most coding work, reach for Opus where its lower error rate (2.1% vs Haiku’s 4.4%) earns its cost, and use Haiku for trivial mechanical tasks.
After model choice, the levers rank in this order. Extended thinking fires on 11.9% of messages and roughly triples output (a 2.53× multiplier), so turn it off for routine work. Hooks on the highest-volume tool matter next. Read is 72% of all tool-output volume, six times Bash, so a hook that truncates Read output beats the well-known Bash-filter recipe. Compaction discipline at task boundaries comes third. Only about 2% of sessions ever compact, and /compact taxes the next request about 3× the dollar cost of /clear, so reach for it for continuity, not cache savings. Keeping a lean baseline (trimming CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, and unused skills) is a real factor, but it adds up to cents per cold session, not dollars. Diagnose before prescribing: run /context all, /usage, and /skills t to find the quiet thing eating your tokens, then run the 30-line Python script at the end on your own logs. Every distribution here is one developer's, and yours will differ.
The article also refutes the most-repeated claims that don’t survive measurement. The “5-minute cache TTL, hurry up” advice is wrong for Claude Code’s stable prefix, where 82.9% of cache writes use the 1-hour TTL. A popular claim says subagents multiply the cost by 5× to 7×. In practice, they cost about the same as a skill: 1.28× on tiny tasks and 0.91× on large ones, with a flat break-even band between roughly 15 and 40 files. The caveman skill advertises "75% savings", but the data says the opposite. It trims words by about 26% and characters by about 23% on screen, yet adds about 22% to the output tokens you are billed for, so the answer looks shorter while the bill does not drop. Claude runs tools one at a time within a turn (only 2 of 18,275 messages did otherwise) and usually stops after an error (54% of errored calls end the chain). Per-turn cost in a long session varies too. It follows a U-shaped curve that is cheapest between turns 9 and 50. One earlier finding of the author ("CLAUDE.md presence inflates output 15%") was explicitly retracted after a cleaner replication. The takeaway: measure your own setup, put your attention on the highest-impact levers first, and stop repeating the unverified savings percentages.

Contents

• Two kinds of readers, two kinds of incentives

• Most of your bill is invisible architecture, not your prompts

• Diagnose before prescribing

• The cache-first worldview (the 5-minute TTL is misleading)

• Lean context: CLAUDE.md, MCP, skills, hooks

• Model and thinking budget — the single biggest cost lever (incl. Caveman skill — measured compression on prose)

• Compaction — three tiers, but most sessions never reach them

• Subagents — when delegation pays off, when it doesn’t

• Prompting and session hygiene

• Anti-patterns to avoid

• A measurement-first cheat sheet

• What we found — answering the question

• What this article didn’t measure

• Methodology appendix — the controlled-experiment harnesses

• Reproduce on your own data

Two kinds of readers, two kinds of incentives
Claude Code has two pricing models, and they shape every recommendation in this article.
If you pay a flat subscription (Pro at $20/month, Max 5× at $100, Max 20× at $200), reducing tokens doesn’t lower your bill. Your bill is the subscription fee. What it does is let you fit more work inside the 5-hour and weekly rate-limit windows the platform enforces. If you pay by API token, every tactic here translates directly to dollars off the next invoice. Both readers benefit from the same techniques for different reasons. I’ll flag where the distinction matters.
Every number below comes from one of three places:

• The official Anthropic Claude Code documentation,

• Prior community write-ups (cited where used),

• A direct measurement of 573 Claude Code session logs from one developer’s ~/.claude/projects/ directory: 28,738 assistant requests in total, spanning January through May 2026.
The Python script that produced everything is at the end so you can reproduce or refute any claim.
A caveat I’ll restate later but want to flag upfront: this is one developer’s logs. The workflow is TypeScript/React-heavy with light MCP usage. The structural mechanisms (caching, model selection, hook leverage) are general. The specific distributions are this developer’s. Your file-extension mix, your tool ratios, and your model split will differ. Run the script at the bottom on your own data to get yours.
The headline percentages dominating the secondary literature (“reduce by 90%”, “cut by 70%”, “save 60% with four habits”) have no methodology behind them. I don’t repeat any. Where my measurements contradict popular advice, I say so explicitly.
What this article asks. Two questions in one. Constructive: What actually reduces token usage on Claude Code? Refutational: Which of the most-repeated tactics in the secondary literature survive a measurement? Every section answers one or the other. Where the two overlap (a popular claim that is also load-bearing for a real recommendation), I show the measurement that resolves it. The article closes by restating both answers in priority order so the reader doesn’t have to assemble them from twelve scattered tables.

Most of your bill is invisible architecture, not your prompts
The conventional framing of “save tokens” is about how you talk to Claude. The data says the opposite: in the average session, your prompt is a rounding error, and the bulk of your tokens were spent before you typed anything.
Anthropic’s own interactive context-window walkthrough shows an illustrative example session start:

• System prompt — ~4,200 tokens

• Auto-memory (MEMORY.md, capped at 200 lines / 25KB) — ~680 tokens

• Environment info — ~280 tokens

• MCP tool names (schemas deferred) — ~120 tokens

• Skill descriptions — ~450 tokens

• ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (global) — ~320 tokens

• Project CLAUDE.md — ~1,800 tokens

• Baseline before your first prompt — ~7,850 tokens
Your typical one-line prompt is around 45 tokens. Roughly 0.5% of what’s already in context.
This explains why prompt-engineering advice has small effect compared with configuration-level changes. One AI content creator, Ruben Hassid, cited a developer who tracked usage and found that 98.5% of tokens were spent re-reading history. The framing is directionally right about the imbalance, though wrong about the cost: most of those re-read tokens are cache hits at 10% pricing, not full-price input. Either way, the lesson stands: the levers worth pulling are not on the prompt side.

Diagnose before prescribing
Before applying any tactic, run /context all command in your session. The official documentation describes it as "a colored grid" showing "optimization suggestions for context-heavy tools, memory bloat, and capacity warnings." Pass all to expand the per-item breakdown, and you'll see which item is consuming what.
If /context says your CLAUDE.md is 5,000 tokens, that's your first action. If it says your MCP tools are loaded at full schemas instead of just names, see the MCP section below. If it says skill descriptions occupy 3K tokens but you only ever invoke two of them, see the skills section.
/usage (aliased as /cost and /stats) shows your session economics. The key numbers: total cost, cache read ratio, current model. Across 28,738 assistant requests in my dataset, the median cache-read ratio is 98.1%. Anything materially below 90% over a session is a configuration smell, not a model issue.
For cross-session analysis, install ccusage (npx ccusage) or write a few lines of Python against ~/.claude/projects/. The data is already on your disk; the script in the appendix produces the numbers cited here.
Three more diagnostic commands worth knowing:

• /skills t: lists installed skills sorted by token cost. Find the heavy ones that you never invoke.

• /mcp: shows MCP servers; disable unused.

• /doctor: flags skill-listing-budget overflow and other configuration issues.
Most “my Claude Code is expensive” stories I’ve read could be resolved in five minutes of looking at /context all. Skip the optimization tour; find the quiet offender first.

The cache-first worldview (the 5-minute TTL is misleading)
Anthropic’s API documentation says the default cache TTL is 5 minutes. In Claude Code, that’s not what most of your cache writes are using. This is one of the more counterintuitive findings of the article.
In the 573-session dataset, 82.9% of all cache writes use the 1-hour TTL variant rather than the 5-minute one. Claude Code’s harness appears to opt into the longer cache for stable prefix content (system prompt, tools, CLAUDE.md, auto-memory), and it does so on its own. There’s no user-facing setting that controls it.
I say “appears” because Anthropic has not publicly documented this as Claude Code default behavior, and the default has demonstrably changed at least once: a community-filed issue (anthropics/claude-code#46829) measured that around March 6-8, 2026, the default silently flipped from 1h to 5m for a period, causing widespread quota inflation reports. The issue was closed as "not planned" without an official Anthropic response. In this user's data, the monthly 1h-share moved through 40% (January), 93% (February), 76% (March transition), 91% (April), 97.7% (May). The current state is back to 1h-dominant, but you cannot rely on it staying that way.
What this means in practice: in current Claude Code, the cache typically does last much longer than the documented 5-minute floor. Empirically, hit ratios in this data hold at 98–99% out to the 30–60 minute bin and only collapse past the 1-hour mark. The “your cache dies in 5 minutes, hurry up” advice in most community articles is repeating the documented default, which doesn’t describe what the harness is currently doing. But because that behavior has flipped before, monitor your own cache hit ratios in /usage; the day yours suddenly drops is the day Anthropic adjusted something.
Practical implications:

• Don’t panic-compact because you stepped away for coffee. The cache survives the bathroom break.

• /compact and /clear are context-management tools, routinely mistaken for cache-savings tools, and the two are very different. A controlled experiment (100 trials, 50 per arm, randomly interleaved with seed=42, identical priming workload in the same working directory, headless claude -p --resume) measured
